lens.included_activities: US multi-person creative studios, production teams, ensembles, and technical-creative firms in the $1-10M normalized EBITDA band that repeatedly deliver commissioned or licensed writing, visual art, performance, recording, restoration, design, or production expertise to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Single-person or celebrity-dependent performance and endorsement vehicles, one-creator royalty or IP holding entities, one-off project companies, individual freelancers without transferable operations, captive units, and financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External publishers, producers, brands, venues, institutions, platforms, and audiences pay project or performance fees, retainers, royalties, licenses, usage fees, or revenue shares for repeated creative output and production expertise.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code explicitly combines freelance actors, writers, visual artists, producers, technicians, restorers, speakers, athletes, and celebrities. To keep one coherent operating-company screen, the lens retains multi-person firms with repeat external work and excludes individual-identity vehicles whose output and customer relationship cannot be separated from one creator or performer.

a.rationale: BLS shows that 57.8% of 711500 employment is in arts, design, entertainment, sports, and media occupations, including 20.3% in writers and editors, 20.8% in entertainers and performers, 9.3% in art and design, and 6.4% in media equipment work; another 8.2% is office support and 9.1% management. Writing, editing, research, concept iteration, image and audio post-production, documentation, promotion, and administration are substantially exposed, while live performance, physical craft, restoration, client discovery, taste, and final creative accountability are less substitutable. The range is a wage-weighted judgment rather than a measured task share.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-linked time studies across a representative sample of LMM writing, visual, performance, restoration, and technical-production firms, with task hours tagged before and after generative text, image, audio, video, and workflow-tool adoption.
caveats: The BLS matrix is employment-weighted, while this primitive is wage-weighted; management and high-paid creative roles may receive more weight than their headcount shares.
O*NET writer tasks illuminate only one part of a highly heterogeneous creative workforce.
Creative assistance and faster iteration do not necessarily substitute labor if customers demand more variants or higher quality.

rho.rationale: Digital creative and office workflows can absorb AI quickly, and IAB reports that three in four surveyed brands use or plan to use AI for creator-marketing tasks. Implementation is constrained by output quality, provenance, copyright, client approval, union rules, creator consent, and the need to preserve a differentiated voice. Census found only 17%-20% of US businesses using AI from late 2025 through early May 2026, showing that available capability does not automatically become operational adoption.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year panel of 711510 firms reporting production deployment by workflow, accepted-output rates, revision time, rights-clearance failures, client restrictions, labor hours avoided, and headcount avoided, segmented by creative vertical and firm size.
caveats: IAB surveyed creator-ad buyers, not 711510 operators, and combines current use with planned use.
Census covers all nonfarm industries and any business-function use, not deep workflow implementation.
Guild restrictions cover important screen and performance work but not all creative firms.

e.rationale: Census defines the industry around independent freelance individuals and lists actors, writers, artists, producers, technicians, speakers, athletes, and celebrities. Even inside the supplied LMM firm band, many apparent firms are likely personal service, touring, endorsement, or IP vehicles whose earnings depend on one named person. Eligibility is limited to the smaller subset with repeat external work, multi-person delivery, firm-owned workflows and customer relationships, and operations that survive creator departure.
e.change_evidence: Entity-level review of the 353 supplied candidates covering active headcount, revenue by creator and customer, contract assignability, recurring versus one-off work, ownership of IP and equipment, second-layer creative leadership, and ability to replace the founder or named performer.
caveats: The supplied n is margin-bridged from employer-firm statistics, but the underlying NAICS definition is centered on freelance individuals.
Legal entities may employ substantial support staffs while remaining economically inseparable from one celebrity or creator.
Eligibility varies sharply among writing studios, live ensembles, restoration shops, technical crews, and personal performance vehicles.

s5.bridge: The source population is 1,162 US business-owner respondents across industries, 49% of whom wanted to exit within five years. This is transformed downward to a qualifying-control-transfer probability because intent does not equal completion, 70% preferred internal options, and creative firms face unusually high founder, name, and IP dependence. Selection into an exit-planning survey likely biases intent upward; the narrowed eligible population is more transferable than the full code, partly offsetting that adjustment.
s5.rationale: Eligible multi-person firms can transact, but an outside control sale is only one exit path and creative-identity risk can prevent completion or turn a sale into a short-lived earnout. The range therefore sits well below broad stated exit intent.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, GF Data, and arts-industry broker records matched to the supplied firm denominator, coding completed control sales, add-ons, closures, internal successions, founder retention, earnouts, and post-close client or talent loss.
caveats: The owner survey is voluntary, cross-industry, and oriented toward exit planning.
Exit intent is not a completed control transfer.
Small creative-company transactions and acqui-hires are often undisclosed or ambiguously classified.

q.rationale: Fixed project, license, usage, performance, and royalty revenue can let an operator retain early production savings, but competitive bidding and renewals reprice routine output, hourly technical work passes time savings to buyers, and royalties or talent fees may not fall when support labor does. Retention is further reduced when customers demand more iterations or when savings fund senior human review, rights clearance, and differentiated creative quality.
q.change_evidence: Five-year contract and job-costing cohorts showing pricing model, gross labor avoided, revision volume, customer renewals, realized margin, royalty or usage-fee changes, and bid rates before and after AI deployment across each retained creative vertical.
caveats: The code mixes fixed-fee, hourly, royalty, licensing, ticket, appearance, and endorsement economics.
Union compensation and AI-consent provisions affect covered work but not the whole industry.
This estimate excludes demand and self-service loss, which are reflected in d5 and o.

d5.bridge: BLS projects 711500 employment from 62,200 in 2024 to 61,200 in 2034, a 1.5% ten-year decline; applying half of that horizon yields a base near 0.99. Employment is a proxy for constant-price, constant-quality service quantity: AI productivity can make employment understate output demand, while creator self-service, unpaid independent work, and changing project intensity can make it overstate demand. NEA's finding that 2023 independent-artist value added was roughly at 2019 but below 2022 and IAB's rapid creator-ad growth justify a wide two-sided range.
d5.rationale: The direct industry employment projection is approximately flat, matching mixed evidence: self-publishing and creator advertising expand addressable demand, while technical self-production, generative content, and weak economics for some legacy arts reduce labor-mediated work. Constant-quality demand is therefore centered near current levels with material vertical dispersion.
d5.change_evidence: BEA-NEA ACPSA annual real-output series for independent artists through 2031, paired with constant-dollar commissioned-work, licensing, booking, performance, restoration, and technical-production volumes for the narrowed multi-person firm population.
caveats: Industry employment is not service quantity and omits self-employed people with no payroll staff in some source systems.
The BLS ten-year projection is converted to five years and covers the full heterogeneous code, not the narrowed lens.
Creator advertising is only one fast-growing slice, while the NEA value-added observation has a 2023 endpoint.

o.rationale: Routine copy, images, audio, video, and technical preparation can be generated or self-served, reducing the quantity that requires a creative firm. Yet copyright protection for AI-assisted output depends on sufficient human-authored expressive elements, WGA-covered literary material cannot treat AI as a writer, and SAG-AFTRA commercial digital replicas require performer consent and compensation. Customers also retain accountable humans for briefs, factual accuracy, taste, rights, live performance, physical craft, restoration, and final approval.
o.change_evidence: Buyer-side procurement data measuring the share of creative briefs completed internally with software versus outsourced to firms, accepted AI-only deliverables, repeat purchase and liability outcomes, plus changes in Copyright Office guidance, guild contracts, and state or federal digital-replica law.
caveats: Copyrightability is not required for every customer use, so human-authorship rules may not preserve all work.
WGA and SAG-AFTRA terms cover union productions and performers, not the entire NAICS code.
The operator requirement is much higher for live, physical, restorative, and high-reputation work than for routine commercial content.

narrative.executive_view: The code contains a large AI-exposed creative and administrative surface, but it is structurally difficult to consolidate because Census defines it around freelancers and many LMM entities remain personal brands or IP vehicles. The investable population is the narrower set of multi-person studios and production businesses whose customer relationships, delivery capacity, and creative standards persist beyond one named individual.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate research, outlining, drafting, editing, storyboards, concept variants, image and audio preparation, metadata, post-production, promotion, scheduling, and back-office work. It is less able to replace live performance, physical craft, restoration, differentiated taste, customer discovery, factual responsibility, rights clearance, and the human authorship or consent needed for important commercial uses.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed-price and licensed work can retain near-term production savings, while hourly assignments and competitively bid routine content pass savings through more quickly. Customers may convert faster production into more variants and revisions, and operators must reinvest in senior review, provenance, security, and rights management.
narrative.firm_availability: Broad owner exit intent does not translate directly into transferable creative firms. Diligence must distinguish organizations with firm-owned brands, contracts, IP processes, archives, equipment, and second-layer leadership from vehicles whose clients, audience, rights, and earnings leave with a founder, celebrity, writer, or performer.
narrative.demand_durability: BLS projects roughly flat industry employment, while NEA shows independent-artist value added around its 2019 level in 2023 and IAB shows rapid growth in creator advertising. These signals support stable aggregate demand with strong internal rotation toward digital, creator, and self-published work and away from some routine or technically intermediated services.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main uncertainties are extreme business-model heterogeneity, personal-identity concentration, unclear contract and IP transferability, rapid quality improvement in generative media, litigation and licensing rules, platform dependence, and customers' ability to self-serve. A bad outcome is buying a seemingly scaled firm that is actually a single creator's income stream just as routine content prices fall.
narrative.principal_driver: A multi-person creative operator can combine exposed digital workflows with human authorship, taste, rights control, and accountable delivery that customers still need.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most of the code is organized around individual creators and performers, making the genuinely transferable firm population much smaller than the supplied firm estimate.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 711510 Independent Artists, Writers, and Performers
sources.supports: Defines the industry as independent freelance individuals performing, creating artistic or cultural works, or providing production expertise, and lists actors, producers, restorers, technicians, artists, speakers, dancers, journalists, writers, athletes, and celebrities.

sources.title: BLS National Employment Matrix: Independent artists, writers, and performers
sources.supports: Reports 62,200 industry jobs in 2024 and 61,200 projected for 2034, a 1.5% decline; provides the industry occupation mix, including 57.8% arts/media and 8.2% office support employment.

sources.title: BLS Factors affecting occupational utilization, projected 2024-2034
sources.supports: Identifies rising self-publishing as support for writers in 711500 and identifies AI or digital technology as increasing productivity or reducing demand for technical writers, audio and video technicians, sound engineers, lighting technicians, and other media work.

sources.title: O*NET 27-3043.00 - Writers and Authors
sources.supports: Lists writing tasks including research, campaign development, varying tone, drafting, editing and rewriting, planning, formatting, client discussion, collaboration, and copyright procedures.

sources.title: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.supports: Reports nationally representative BTOS AI use of 17%-20% from December 2025 through May 2026, expected use of 20%-23%, and less than 20% use among firms with four or fewer employees.

sources.title: U.S. Copyright Office Releases Part 2 of Artificial Intelligence Report
sources.supports: States that AI outputs are copyrightable only where a human author determines sufficient expressive elements, that prompting alone is insufficient, and that assistive AI does not bar copyrightability.

sources.title: Writers Guild of America West: Artificial Intelligence
sources.supports: States that AI is not a writer under the 2023 MBA, AI-generated output is not literary material, writers may choose to use AI with company consent, and companies must disclose AI-generated materials.

sources.title: SAG-AFTRA 2025 Commercials Contracts
sources.supports: Requires consent before creating a digital replica and informed consent before use, and provides a 1.5-times session fee plus applicable holding and use fees for replica-generated performance.

sources.title: NEA: Arts and Cultural Industries Grew at Twice the Rate of the U.S. Economy
sources.supports: Reports that independent artists, writers, and performers had 2023 value added roughly on par with 2019 but below 2022, and notes that ACPSA payroll employment excludes self-employed arts workers without staff.

sources.title: 2023 National State of Owner Readiness Report
sources.supports: Surveyed 1,162 US business owners; 49% wanted to exit within five years, 70% preferred an internal option, and 42% had a written formal business transition plan.

sources.title: IAB 2025 Creator Economy Ad Spend & Strategy Report
sources.supports: Reports US creator ad spend of $37 billion in 2025, up 26% year over year, expected to reach $44 billion in 2026, and says three in four surveyed brands use or plan to use AI for creator-marketing tasks.
